blocks.html:
Claude is a family of models on a capability/cost/latency spectrum: Haiku (fastest, cheapest — classification, routing, simple high-volume steps), Sonnet (the balanced production workhorse), and Opus (most capable — complex agentic work and hard analysis). Versions and prices change; the patterns don't.

Every question in this section is an allocation question: match the tier to the step, not the product. A support pipeline might classify with a small model, converse with a mid-tier one, and reserve the top tier for escalations triggered by measurable signals — low calibrated confidence, failed validation, an explicit "needs deeper analysis" flag. Defaulting everything to the biggest model "to be safe" is a budget decision dressed up as a safety decision. And a capable coordinator can delegate focused subtasks to cheaper models — narrow scope shrinks the capability gap. Validate any tier change with a segmented eval set, not vibes.

Thinking is adaptive on current models: the model decides when and how deeply to reason, scaled by a per-request effort setting (fixed thinking-token budgets are legacy). Spend effort where the task is reasoning-shaped — planning a migration, reconciling ambiguous requirements, debugging from indirect evidence. Mechanical extraction doesn't benefit; buy accuracy there with schemas and few-shot examples. Streaming is a delivery choice, not a quality one: stream when a human is watching (time-to-first-token = perceived speed) or outputs are long (timeout risk); skip it for machine-to-machine calls.

Production code must branch on stop_reason:

  • end_turn — done. tool_use — run the tools and continue. stop_sequence — expected if configured.
  • max_tokens — the OUTPUT hit your cap: raise it, stream, or split. model_context_window_exceeded — the INPUT doesn't fit: compact/trim/summarize. Different limits, different fixes; log which occurred.
  • pause_turn — re-send the conversation as-is; a server-side operation resumes.
  • refusal — surface or route to review; never blind-retry.

API-level errors: 429 → honor retry-after, exponential backoff + jitter (the SDKs already do this — don't hand-roll); 500/529 → transient, retry; 400 → malformed, fix the request. Sustained 429s are a capacity-planning signal: spread load, move deferrable volume to batch, request higher limits, split across models — remembering limits are per model and counted in tokens as well as requests. For budgeting, use the free token-counting endpoint; third-party tokenizers miscount Claude tokens.

items: Allocate tiers per step; escalate on measurable signal, not anxiety.
Effort is a per-request dial — spend it on reasoning-shaped work only.
Stream for watching humans and long outputs; it changes delivery, not quality.
max_tokens = your output cap; context_window_exceeded = input overflow. Branch on stop_reason.
Sustained 429s mean capacity planning, not better retry loops.
